The temple’s porters and treasurers needed strength and bravery to stop people who wrongly tried to enter the sanctuary and to protect the sacred treasures. A lot was spent daily on the altar: flour, wine, oil, salt, fuel, and lamps; large amounts of these were kept in advance, along with the sacred vestments and utensils. These were the treasures of God’s house. These treasures showed the abundance there is in our heavenly Father's house, enough and to spare. From those sacred treasuries, the unsearchable riches of Christ supply all our needs; and receiving from his fullness, we must give him glory and try to use our abilities and possessions according to his will. We have an account of those employed as officers and judges. The magistracy is God’s ordinance for the good of the church, just as truly as the ministry, and it must not be neglected. None of the Levites who were employed in the service of the sanctuary, none of the singers or porters, were involved in this external business; one duty was enough to engage the whole person. Wisdom, courage, strength of faith, holy affections, and constancy of mind in doing our duty are necessary for every position.
